questions.questionText: Endometriosis is defined as the presence of:
questions.optionB: Functional endometrial tissue (glands and stroma) outside the uterine cavity — most commonly on ovaries (chocolate cyst/endometrioma)
questions.explanation: Endometriosis: ectopic endometrial tissue (glands + stroma). Most common site: ovaries (endometrioma/chocolate cyst — blood-filled cysts). Other sites: pouch of Douglas, uterosacral ligaments, fallopian tubes, bladder, rectum. Symptoms: chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, infertility, dyschezia (painful defecation). Diagnosis: laparoscopy (gold standard — powder burn/gunshot lesions). CA-125: elevated but non-specific. Treatment: NSAIDs, hormonal (OCP, GnRH agonists, dienogest), surgery (excision/ablation). Reference: DC Dutta — Endometriosis.

questions.questionText: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is characterized by (Rotterdam criteria — need 2 of 3):
questions.optionB: Oligo/anovulation, clinical/biochemical hyperandrogenism, and polycystic ovaries on USG (≥12 follicles 2-9mm or volume >10mL)
questions.explanation: PCOS (Rotterdam 2003): need 2 of 3 — (1) Oligo/anovulation (irregular periods), (2) Clinical hyperandrogenism (hirsutism, acne, alopecia) or biochemical (elevated free testosterone), (3) Polycystic ovaries on USG (≥12 follicles 2-9mm in either ovary OR volume >10mL). Most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women. Associated: insulin resistance, obesity, metabolic syndrome, type 2 DM, infertility. LH:FSH ratio >2:1. Treatment: lifestyle, metformin (insulin resistance), OCP (regularize cycles), clomiphene (ovulation induction). Reference: DC Dutta — PCOS.

questions.questionText: The most common pelvic organ prolapse is:
questions.optionB: Cystocele (anterior vaginal wall prolapse with descent of bladder)
questions.explanation: Cystocele: most common pelvic organ prolapse — descent of bladder through anterior vaginal wall. Risk factors: multiparity, vaginal delivery, menopause (estrogen loss), chronic straining, obesity. POP-Q staging: standardized quantification. Uterovaginal prolapse degrees: 1st (cervix within vagina), 2nd (cervix at introitus), 3rd (cervix outside introitus), procidentia (complete eversion). Treatment: Kegel exercises, pessary (non-surgical), pelvic floor repair, hysterectomy (definitive for uterine prolapse). Reference: DC Dutta — Pelvic Organ Prolapse.
